- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init
- review
- security-review

1	# Agent Nervous System
2	
3	### Goal-Directed Perception for AI Browser Agents
4	
5	\---
6	
7	## The Core Idea
8	
9	AI agents that browse the web are fundamentally blind. They rely on text descriptions of pages, accessibility trees, and raw HTML — none of which tell them what the screen actually looks like, what's blocking their path, or whether the last action they took actually worked.
10	
11	The standard fix has been: give agents a screenshot and a vision model. But that's not enough. Having eyes is not the same as seeing with purpose.
12	
13	**Agent Nervous System** is a perception, decision, and verification architecture that gives AI agents not just eyes — but goal-directed eyes, connected to each other through a shared nervous system, so they always work toward the same objective and never get pulled off course by distractions.
14	
15	\---
16	
17	## The Problem
18	
19	### Agents Are Blind by Design
20	
21	When a browser agent navigates a website, it typically works like this:
22	
23	```
24	Agent sends command → Browser executes → Returns text/HTML → Agent guesses what happened
25	```
26	
27	There is no visual confirmation. No verification that the action worked. No awareness of what's visually blocking the page. The agent is operating in the dark and hoping the HTML tells the truth.
28	
29	### The Failure Loop
30	
31	```
32	Agent tries to click a button
33	        ↓
34	Popup appeared covering the button (agent doesn't know)
35	        ↓
36	Click fails silently
37	        ↓
38	Agent retries the same click
39	        ↓
40	Fails again
41	        ↓
42	Agent gives up or halts
43	```
44	
45	This is not a rare edge case. Popups, cookie banners, newsletter modals, ad overlays, and redirects happen on almost every modern website. Agents fail constantly because they have no immune system against distractions.
46	
47	### The Three Core Failures
48	
49	|Failure|What Happens|Why It Happens|
50	|-|-|-|
51	|**Blind Execution**|Agent acts on stale or wrong page state|No visual confirmation|
52	|**Distraction Collapse**|Agent gets sidetracked by popups, ads, banners|No goal-signal during perception|
53	|**Silent Failure**|Action completes but goal didn't advance|No semantic verification layer|
54	
55	\---
56	
57	## The Insight: Agents Can Rent Eyes
58	
59	A blind human has one fundamental constraint — eyes are biologically attached to a person. You cannot borrow someone else's vision.
60	
61	An AI agent has no such limitation. It is software. It can receive any type of input from any source simultaneously. This means an agent can:
62	
63	* Rent a DOM reader as one eye
64	* Rent a vision model as another eye
65	* Rent a goal verifier as a third eye
66	* Run all of them at the same time
67	
68	**But renting eyes alone is not enough.** Most current systems that use vision models still fail, because the eyes are unguided. They describe everything equally — the popup, the footer, the cookie banner, the actual content — with no sense of what matters.
69	
70	The real insight is:
71	
72	> Give agents goal-directed eyes. Connect those eyes to a shared nervous system. Let every eye see what every other eye sees, and let the goal signal flow through all of them continuously.
73	
74	That is what this system builds.
75	
76	\---
77	
78	## What Existing Solutions Get Right (and Wrong)
79	
80	### Vercel agent-browser (33.1k stars)
81	
82	A browser automation CLI built in Rust. Extremely fast. Keeps Chrome alive between commands. Provides powerful low-level primitives.
83	
84	**What it does well:**
85	
86	* Native binary performance
87	* Stable browser session management
88	* `diff snapshot` — compare page states before and after an action
89	
90	**What's missing:**
91	
92	* The diff tells you *what* changed, not *whether that change meant the goal advanced*
93	* No goal awareness anywhere in the pipeline
94	* A dumb executor — no decision layer asking "should I take this action?"
95	
96	### Agent-E (1.2k stars)
97	
98	Built on AutoGen. Uses a planner agent and a browser navigation agent. Introduced DOM Distillation.
99	
100	**What it does well:**
101	
102	* DOM Distillation — strips raw HTML down to only semantically meaningful elements
103	* Three distillation modes: text-only, input fields, all-fields
104	* Dual-agent structure separates planning from execution
105	
106	**What's missing:**
107	
108	* Distillation is static — doesn't change mode based on goal stage
109	* Action verification detects DOM changes but not semantic goal advancement
110	* No cross-agent perception — planner and navigator don't share a live perceptual state
111	
112	### The Gap Both Leave Open
113	
114	```
115	What both tools do:           What neither tool does:
116	────────────────────────      ────────────────────────────────
117	Detect DOM changed      →     "Did this change mean goal advanced?"
118	Retry on thrown error   →     "WHY did it fail? What type of failure?"
119	Take actions            →     "SHOULD I take this action right now?"
120	See the page            →     "Is what I'm seeing relevant to my goal?"
121	```
122	
123	\---
124	
125	## The Solution: Two Borrowed Features + One New Layer
126	
127	### Feature 1: DOM Distillation (from Agent-E)
128	
129	Instead of passing raw HTML to the agent, strip the DOM down to only what matters. Three modes:
130	
131	* **Text-only** — for reading content and search results
132	* **Input fields** — for form interaction and data entry
133	* **All-fields** — for comprehensive page understanding
134	
135	The nervous system selects the mode dynamically based on the current goal stage. Searching for a flight uses input fields mode. Reading results uses text-only mode.
136	
137	### Feature 2: Compare Page States (from Vercel agent-browser)
138	
139	After every action, diff the page state before and after. Know exactly what changed.
140	
141	**The key upgrade:** By running the diff on distilled DOM rather than raw HTML, the comparison is clean. No noise from ad containers reloading, timestamps ticking, or cookie scripts firing. Only semantically meaningful elements are compared.
142	
143	```
144	Raw DOM → DOM Distillation → Cleaned DOM
145	                                  │
146	                         Compare page states
147	                         (diffing signal, not noise)
148	                                  │
149	                    Decision Intelligence Layer
150	                    "Did this diff mean the goal advanced?"
151	```
152	
153	### Feature 3: Decision Intelligence Layer (new)
154	
155	The layer that neither repo has. After every action, after every diff, this layer asks:
156	
157	* Did the page change in a way that advances the goal?
158	* If yes — what is the next step?
159	* If no — why not? What type of failure occurred?
160	* What should the agent do differently?
161	
162	This is the difference between detecting a change and understanding it.
163	
164	\---
165	
166	## The Architecture
167	
168	### The Nervous System
169	
170	The central innovation is not the individual eyes. It is the shared nervous system that connects them.
171	
172	```
173	┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
174	│                   NERVOUS SYSTEM                     │
175	│                                                      │
176	│  ┌──────────────────┐   ┌────────────────────────┐   │
177	│  │  Goal State      │   │  Shared Memory         │   │
178	│  │  (broadcast      │   │  (what all eyes have   │   │
179	│  │   continuously)  │   │   seen and reported)   │   │
180	│  └──────────────────┘   └────────────────────────┘   │
181	│                                                      │
182	│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
183	│  │  Signal Router                                 │  │
184	│  │  Eye reports → relevance scored against goal   │  │
185	│  │  → suppressed (distraction) or amplified       │  │
186	│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
187	│                                                      │
188	│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
189	│  │  Distraction Classifier                        │  │
190	│  │  Ad / Popup / Banner / Redirect / Modal        │  │
191	│  │  → immune response triggered automatically     │  │
192	│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
193	│                                                      │
194	│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
195	│  │  Failure Classifier                            │  │
196	│  │  Silent fail / Wrong element / Blocked /       │  │
197	│  │  State mismatch / Goal drift                   │  │
198	│  │  → specific recovery strategy per type         │  │
199	│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
200	└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
201	```
202	
203	### The Eyes
204	
205	Each eye is a separate perception module. All are connected to the nervous system. All receive the goal signal continuously.
206	
207	```
208	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
209	│                      EYES LAYER                         │
210	│                                                         │
211	│  Eye 1 — DOM Reader                                     │
212	│  Source: Agent-E DOM Distillation                       │
213	│  Reports: Interactive elements, input fields, content   │
214	│  Mode: Selected by nervous system based on goal stage   │
215	│                                                         │
216	│  Eye 2 — Vision Model                                   │
217	│  Source: Screenshot → Claude / GPT-4o                   │
218	│  Reports: Visual layout, overlays, blocked regions      │
219	│  Mode: Pixel-level understanding of actual screen       │
220	│                                                         │
221	│  Eye 3 — Page Diff                                      │
222	│  Source: Vercel agent-browser compare page states       │
223	│  Reports: What changed between action before/after      │
224	│  Mode: Runs on distilled DOM, not raw HTML              │
225	│                                                         │
226	│  Eye 4 — Goal Verifier                                  │
227	│  Source: Decision Intelligence Layer                    │
228	│  Reports: Did the last action advance the goal?         │
229	│  Mode: Semantic verification, not just change detection │
230	│                                                         │
231	│  Eye 5 — Error Detector                                 │
232	│  Source: DOM + Vision combined                          │
233	│  Reports: Visual errors, failed states, wrong pages     │
234	│  Mode: Failure classification with recovery strategy    │
235	│                                                         │
236	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
237	```
238	
239	### Cross-Eye Awareness
240	
241	Each eye sees what every other eye reports. This is what humans cannot do — and it is why the system can reason about situations no individual perception channel could resolve alone.
242	
243	```
244	Example: Newsletter popup appears mid-booking
245	
246	Eye 1 (DOM):    "I see a Subscribe button and a Close button"
247	Eye 2 (Vision): "I see a dark overlay covering the main content"
248	Eye 3 (Diff):   "New elements appeared since last action"
249	Eye 4 (Verify): "Goal state has not advanced"
250	Eye 5 (Error):  "Interaction blocked — element not reachable"
251	
252	Nervous System synthesizes:
253	→ A popup appeared (Eye 3)
254	→ It is visually blocking content (Eye 2)
255	→ It has a dismissible action (Eye 1)
256	→ Goal is not advancing (Eye 4)
257	→ Primary flow is blocked (Eye 5)
258	→ Conclusion: Fire immune response, close popup, resume goal
259	```
260	
261	No single eye reaches this conclusion. The nervous system does.
262	
263	\---
264	
265	## The Immune System
266	
267	When a distraction appears — ad, popup, cookie banner, newsletter modal, redirect — the immune system fires before any eye's brain acts on it.
268	
269	```
270	Standard agent:                    This system:
271	
272	Distraction appears                Distraction appears
273	        ↓                                  ↓
274	Agent perceives it                 Nervous system intercepts
275	        ↓                                  ↓
276	Agent confused                     Goal pulse checked
277	        ↓                                  ↓
278	Agent acts on distraction          Relevance score: near zero
279	        ↓                                  ↓
280	Agent goes off track               Immune response fires
281	                                           ↓
282	                                   Eyes stay on goal
283	                                           ↓
284	                                   Distraction dismissed
285	                                           ↓
286	                                   Goal flow resumed
287	```
288	
289	The immune response is not the agent learning to ignore things. It is the goal signal being structurally stronger than any distraction signal that enters the system.
290

393	\---
394	
395	## The Superpower Humans Don't Have
396	
397	A human uses one pair of eyes, sequentially. They can only look at one thing at a time. Their brain filters what they attend to — but that filtering is also sequential.
398	
399	This system runs five types of perception simultaneously and synthesizes them into one unified understanding. The information flows laterally between eyes — each eye sees what others report — and the goal signal flows downward continuously through all of them.
400	
401	That is not a metaphor. That is the architecture.
402	
403	No human brain works this way. And no existing browser agent framework is built this way either.
404	
405	\---
406	
407	## What This System Is Not
408	
409	* It is **not** a new browser. It uses existing browser infrastructure.
410	* It is **not** competing with Vercel agent-browser or Agent-E. It sits above them and uses their best primitives.
411	* It is **not** just a multi-agent system. Multi-agent systems have agents reporting up to a coordinator. This system has eyes sharing state laterally and the goal flowing down continuously.
412	
413	\---
414	
415	## Summary
416	
417	> An AI agent with goal-directed eyes, connected through a shared nervous system, where every eye sees what every other eye sees, every action is semantically verified, every failure is classified and recovered from, and no distraction is loud enough to drown out the goal signal.
418	
419	Not just a browser agent. A **perceiving, deciding, verifying system** that knows what it's doing and why.
420

72	## Language Split
73	
74	| Language | Role | What It Owns |
75	|----------|------|-------------|
76	| **Rust** | System Skeleton | AgentWeb Core, DOM Distillation, Page Diff, Immune System, Goal State Manager, Signal Router, IPC Bridge (gRPC server), LanceDB storage |

80	### Why Rust for Infrastructure
81	- Zero-cost abstractions — DOM distillation and page diff run on every page load
82	- Memory safety without GC — goal state shared across parallel sessions
83	- Long-running stability — daemon process runs for hours/days
84	- Fearless concurrency — 5 eyes read shared goal state simultaneously
85	
86	### Why Python for Intelligence
87	- LLM ecosystem — Anthropic SDK, OpenAI SDK are Python-first
88	- API call latency (200ms-3s) dwarfs Python overhead
89	- Rapid prompt iteration — vision prompts, verification prompts evolve weekly
90	- AsyncIO handles I/O-bound parallelism well
91	
92	### Why NOT TypeScript in Critical Path
93	- The agent does not interact with TypeScript code
94	- If TypeScript fails, the agent keeps running
95	- TypeScript is for developer observability only

10	
11	**What v2 adds:**
12	- WASM-compiled ML model (ONNX runtime or burn.rs) loaded into the Rust immune pipeline
13	- Trained on labeled distraction samples collected from v1 heuristic decisions
14	- Replaces/supplements heuristic rules for edge cases the regex patterns miss
15	- Same `<10ms` latency budget, same `ImmuneClassifier` struct (field already reserved)
16	
17	**Trigger:** Collect 50,000+ labeled (page_content, distraction_type) samples from v1 heuristic classifier running in production. Heuristic labels are the training data. When model accuracy exceeds heuristic on held-out test set, ship WASM model.
18	
19	**Crate:** `ans-immune` (same crate, feature-gated)
20	
21	---
22	
23	## 2. NATS Message Broker (Multi-Node)
24	
25	**Status in v1:** Single-node only. `tokio::broadcast` for internal pub/sub. gRPC streaming for Python↔Rust event delivery. Zero external message broker.
26	
27	**What v2 adds:**
28	- NATS JetStream for cross-node pub/sub
29	- Multiple daemon instances sharing goal state via NATS
30	- Distributed eye processing (Vision Eye runs on GPU node, DOM Eye on browser node)
31	- Transparent migration — all subscribers already consume via gRPC streaming interface. Only daemon internals change (`tokio::broadcast` → NATS client)
32	
33	**Trigger:** Need to scale beyond a single machine. Either: (a) >50 concurrent browser sessions saturating one machine, or (b) GPU-heavy vision processing needs separate hardware.
34	
35	**Architecture change:**
36	```
37	v1:  tokio::broadcast ──→ gRPC streaming ──→ Python subscribers
38	v2:  NATS JetStream ──→ gRPC streaming (multi-node) ──→ Python subscribers
39	```
40	
41	---
42	
43	## 3. Servo Browser Engine (Alternative Backend)
44	
45	**Status in v1:** Chromium only via CDP. `BrowserBackend` trait exists but only `CdpBackend` implemented.
46	
47	**What v2 adds:**
48	- `ServoBackend` implementing the same `BrowserBackend` trait
49	- Servo for lightweight pages (documentation, forms, search results, no heavy JS)
50	- Chromium reserved for complex JS-heavy sites
51	- Automatic engine selection based on page characteristics (or agent preference)
52	- Lower memory per session (Servo ~50MB vs Chromium ~200MB)
53	
54	**Trigger:** 100+ concurrent sessions where Chromium memory becomes the bottleneck. Servo's embedding API matures (currently experimental).
55	
56	**Crate:** `ans-servo` (new, feature-gated)
57	
58	---
59	
60	## 4. Firefox / WebKit Backend
61	
62	**Status in v1:** Not started. `BrowserBackend` trait designed for it, but zero implementation.
63	
64	**What v2 adds:**
65	- `FirefoxBackend` via Firefox's CDP-compatible remote debugging protocol
66	- `WebKitBackend` via WebKit's remote inspector protocol (for macOS Safari testing)
67	- Cross-browser testing scenarios (agent verifies site works in Chrome + Firefox + Safari)
68	
69	**Trigger:** Enterprise requirement for cross-browser testing. Or CDP protocol churn makes Chromium-only risky (mitigation in risk matrix).
70	
71	---
72	
73	## 5. Dashboard (Developer UI)
74	
75	**Status in v1:** Not built. Explicitly marked "optional, non-critical" in the architecture. Zero impact on critical path.
76	
77	**What v2 adds:**
78	- **Live Session View:** Real-time display of agent sessions — what page, what goal, what action just executed, what the eyes see
79	- **Injection Alert Dashboard:** Every prompt injection detection with source page, flagged content, action taken (sanitized/blocked), severity score. Red-team audit trail
80	- **Decision Timeline:** Chronological view of every action scored across all 3 business outcome layers (Immediate/Short-Term/Long-Term). See which actions advance goals and which don't
81	- **Budget Monitor:** Per-goal and per-API-key spend tracking. Circuit breaker mode transitions visualized. Historical cost data
82	- **Session Replay:** Step-by-step replay of completed sessions with DOM snapshots, eye reports, and decision scores overlaid
83	- **Goal Analytics:** Aggregate stats — average actions per goal, success rate, most common failure patterns, cost per goal type
84	
85	**Tech:** Next.js + gRPC-web via Envoy proxy → Rust daemon (read-only). Zero write path — dashboard cannot control agents, only observe.
86	
87	**Trigger:** Need human visibility into agent behavior. First user complaint of "I don't know what my agent is doing." Dashboard is the answer.
88	
89	---
90	
91	## 6. CLI Tools (`ans` CLI)
92	
93	**Status in v1:** Not built. Phase 8 alongside dashboard.
94	
95	**What v2 adds:**
96	```
97	ans session list          # List active sessions with goal, page, status
98	ans session inspect <id>  # Live view of one session (what it sees, what it did)
99	ans goal status <id>      # Progress, sub-goals completed, remaining
100	ans decisions query "..."  # Search scored decision history
101	ans immune alerts          # Recent distraction/injection events
102	ans budget status          # Per-goal and total spend
103	ans agent connect          # Connect external agent via MCP (one-shot setup)
104	ans config validate        # Validate ans.toml
105	```
106	
107	**Trigger:** Power users who don't want a browser dashboard. DevOps/headless-server use case.
108	
109	---
110	
111	## 7. Debug Visualizer
112	
113	**Status in v1:** Not built.
114	
115	**What v2 adds:**
116	- Visual overlay showing what each eye perceives on a page
117	- DOM tree with distillation annotations (what was kept, what was removed, why)
118	- Page diff side-by-side with highlighted changes the Goal Verifier saw
119	- Immune system overlay showing elements classified as distractions + injection attempts
120	- Content boundary markers visualized on the rendered page
121	
122	**Trigger:** Debugging why an agent made a wrong decision. "Show me what the agent saw" is the core question.
123	
124	---
125	
126	## 8. Multi-Tenant Auth & Team Features
127	
128	**Status in v1:** API key auth only. Single machine, single user. `api_keys.json` file with scoped permissions.
129	
130	**What v2 adds:**
131	- OAuth2 / OIDC authentication (Google, GitHub, enterprise SSO)
132	- Team workspaces — multiple users sharing agent sessions, decision history, budgets
133	- Role-based access: Admin (full control), Operator (run agents, view dashboard), Viewer (read-only dashboard)
134	- Per-user API key management with usage quotas
135	- Audit log — who ran what agent, when, what it cost
136	
137	**Trigger:** Team adoption. First request for "my colleague needs to see my agent's sessions."
138	
139	---
140	
141	## 9. External Webhook/Callback System
142	
143	**Status in v1:** Short-term and long-term business outcomes defined in the 3-layer model but no automated collection. Short/long-term outcomes updated manually or via LLM fallback evaluation.
144	
145	**What v2 adds:**
146	- Webhook registration API — external systems register callbacks for goal outcome events
147	- Goal Verifier triggers webhooks on sub-goal completion
148	- External callback → LanceDB record update with real business outcome data
149	- Supported outcomes: CI/CD pass/fail, customer reply sentiment, ticket resolution, deployment status, revenue events
150	- Webhook signature verification (HMAC)
151	
152	**Trigger:** Need real business outcome data instead of LLM-estimated outcomes. First integration with CI/CD, CRM, or support desk.
153	
154	---
155	
156	## 10. Custom Eye Plugin System
157	
158	**Status in v1:** Eyes capped at 5 (DOM Reader, Vision Model, Page Diff, Goal Verifier, Error Detector). Hard architecture gate.
159	
160	**What v2 adds:**
161	- Eye trait/interface for third-party eyes
162	- Plugin registry — eyes register capabilities and subscribe to event streams
163	- Community eyes: Accessibility Checker, SEO Analyzer, Performance Profiler, Legal/Compliance Scanner
164	- Eye marketplace in dashboard
165	
166	**Trigger:** User demand for domain-specific eyes beyond the built-in 5. First external eye contributed.
167	
168	---
169	
170	## 11. Cloud Deployment (Managed Service)
171	
172	**Status in v1:** Local daemon only. User runs `ans-daemon` on their machine. No cloud component.
173	
174	**What v2 adds:**
175	- Managed cloud offering — hosted daemon with pooled Chromium instances
176	- Elastic scaling (auto-spawn browser instances as session count grows)
177	- Centralized decision history across all users (federated learning potential)
178	- Usage-based billing (per session-hour, per LLM token, per vision API call)
179	- SOC2 compliance, data residency controls
180	
181	**Trigger:** Users who don't want to run infrastructure locally. Enterprise procurement requirement.
182	
183	---
184	
185	## 12. Advanced Browser Features
186	
187	**Status in v1:** Basic CDP — navigate, click, type, screenshot, extract DOM. One Chromium instance per session.
188	
189	**What v2 adds:**
190	- Network interception (block trackers, analytics, ads at CDP level before DOM distillation)
191	- Cookie/session persistence across browser restarts (maintain login state)
192	- File download handling
193	- iframe isolation (treat cross-origin iframes as separate security boundaries)
194	- Browser profile management (pre-configured profiles with extensions, cookies, preferences)
195	- Mobile device emulation (CDP device metrics override)
196	- Geolocation / timezone spoofing
197	
198	**Trigger:** Agent needs to interact with authenticated services, download files, or test mobile views.
199	
200	---
201	
202	## 13. Decision Intelligence — Advanced Scoring
203	
204	**Status in v1:** Cosine similarity vector search on (action, tool, context). 3-layer business outcome model. k-NN retrieval from LanceDB.
205	
206	**What v2 adds:**
207	- Cross-goal pattern transfer — patterns learned from "book a flight" goal inform "book a hotel" goal
208	- Collaborative scoring — scores from one agent's decisions influence another agent facing similar context
209	- A/B decision testing — agent tries two different actions, compares outcomes, updates scoring
210	- Time-decay weighting — recent outcomes weighted higher than old outcomes
211	- Anomaly detection on decision patterns (flag when agent behavior changes unexpectedly)
212	
213	**Trigger:** Decision store reaches 1M+ records. Cross-goal pattern transfer becomes feasible.
214	
215	---
216	
217	## 14. Extended Language & Framework Support
218	
219	**Status in v1:** Rust daemon + Python intelligence workers. TypeScript dashboard (non-critical). gRPC + Arrow IPC.
220	
221	**What v2 adds:**
222	- Go SDK for external agents (generate Go gRPC client from proto)
223	- Java/Kotlin SDK (JVM ecosystem)
224	- REST API parity with gRPC (all gRPC endpoints exposed via REST for non-gRPC clients)
225	- Webhook output connectors (Slack, PagerDuty, Datadog — agent outcomes surface to ops tools)
226	
227	---
228	
229	## Summary Timeline
230	
231	| Priority | Item | When | Effort |
232	|----------|------|------|--------|
233	| P0 | WASM ML Immune System | After 50K labeled samples collected | 4-6 weeks |
234	| P0 | NATS Multi-Node | When >50 concurrent sessions needed | 3-4 weeks |
235	| P1 | Dashboard | First user visibility request | 4-6 weeks |
236	| P1 | CLI Tools | Alongside dashboard | 2-3 weeks |
237	| P1 | Webhook/Callback System | First external integration | 2-3 weeks |
238	| P2 | Servo Backend | 100+ session scale requirement | 6-8 weeks |
239	| P2 | Debug Visualizer | After dashboard | 3-4 weeks |
240	| P2 | Multi-Tenant Auth | First team adoption | 3-4 weeks |
241	| P2 | Custom Eye Plugins | First community contribution | 4-6 weeks |
242	| P3 | Firefox/WebKit Backend | Enterprise cross-browser requirement | 6-8 weeks |
243	| P3 | Cloud Deployment | Enterprise procurement demand | 12-16 weeks |
244	| P3 | Advanced Browser Features | Authenticated service interaction | 8-12 weeks |
245	| P3 | Advanced Decision Scoring | 1M+ decision records | 6-8 weeks |
246	| P3 | Extended SDKs | External adoption demand | 4-6 weeks |
247	
248	---
249	
250	## Architecture Gates (unchanged from v1)
251	
252	These architectural decisions from v1 remain hard gates — they protect against scope creep:
253	
254	- `BrowserBackend` trait exists but only `CdpBackend` is v1
255	- Eyes capped at 5 (no plugin system in v1)
256	- No WASM ML in immune system (heuristic-only)
257	- No NATS (single-node tokio::broadcast)
258	- TypeScript zero in critical path (dashboard/CLI/debugger only)
259	- gRPC + Arrow IPC remains the only Rust↔Python bridge

1	name: CI
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	    branches: [main]
8	
9	env:
10	  CARGO_TERM_COLOR: always
11	  RUSTFLAGS: "-D warnings"
12	
13	jobs:
14	  # ── Rust: Build, Test, Lint ──────────────────────────────
15	  rust:
16	    name: Rust (${{ matrix.os }})
17	    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
18	    strategy:
19	      matrix:
20	        os: [ubuntu-24.04, macos-14, windows-2025]
21	    steps:
22	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
23	
24	      - name: Install Rust toolchain
25	        uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable
26	        with:
27	          components: rustfmt, clippy
28	
29	      - name: Cache cargo
30	        uses: actions/cache@v4
31	        with:
32	          path: |
33	            ~/.cargo/registry
34	            ~/.cargo/git
35	            target
36	          key: ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-${{ hashFiles('Cargo.lock') }}
37	          restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-
38	
39	      - name: Install protoc
40	        uses: arduino/setup-protoc@v3
41	        with:
42	          version: "27.x"
43	          repo-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
44	
45	      - name: Check formatting
46	        run: cargo fmt --all -- --check
47	
48	      - name: Build workspace
49	        run: cargo build --workspace --verbose
50	
51	      - name: Run clippy
52	        run: cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings
53	
54	      - name: Run tests
55	        run: cargo test --workspace --verbose
56	
57	  # ── Python: Test, Lint ───────────────────────────────────
58	  python:
59	    name: Python (${{ matrix.os }})
60	    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
61	    strategy:
62	      matrix:
63	        os: [ubuntu-24.04, macos-14, windows-2025]
64	    defaults:
65	      run:
66	        working-directory: nerves
67	    steps:
68	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
69	
70	      - name: Install uv
71	        uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5
72	
73	      - name: Install Python
74	        run: uv python install 3.11
75	
76	      - name: Install dependencies
77	        run: uv pip install --system -e ".[dev]"
78	
79	      - name: Run ruff
80	        run: ruff check ans_nerves/ tests/
81	
82	      - name: Run mypy
83	        run: mypy ans_nerves/
84	
85	      - name: Run tests
86	        run: pytest -v
87	
88	  # ── Proto: Verify compilation ────────────────────────────
89	  proto:
90	    name: Proto compilation check
91	    runs-on: ubuntu-24.04
92	    steps:
93	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
94	
95	      - name: Install protoc
96	        uses: arduino/setup-protoc@v3
97	        with:
98	          version: "27.x"
99	          repo-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
100	
101	      - name: Verify proto compiles
102	        run: |
103	          protoc --proto_path=proto proto/ans.proto --descriptor_set_out=/dev/null
104	          echo "Proto compilation successful"
105	
106	  # ── All green gate ───────────────────────────────────────
107	  all-green:
108	    name: All CI checks passed
109	    needs: [rust, python, proto]
110	    runs-on: ubuntu-24.04
111	    steps:
112	      - run: echo "All CI checks passed"

1	"""Decision scoring engine implementation."""
2	
3	from dataclasses import dataclass
4	
5	
6	@dataclass
7	class ImmediateOutcome:
8	    """t=0: Technical success/failure."""
9	    action_succeeded: bool = False
10	    results_produced: str = ""
11	    error_message: str | None = None
12	    execution_time_ms: int = 0
13	
14	    def score(self) -> float:
15	        if self.action_succeeded:
16	            return 1.0
17	        if self.error_message and "timeout" in self.error_message:
18	            return 0.2
19	        return 0.0
20	
21	
22	@dataclass
23	class ShortTermOutcome:
24	    """t=minutes-hours: Session-level goal advancement."""
25	    goal_advanced: bool = False
26	    criterion_met: str | None = None
27	    sub_goal_completed: bool = False
28	
29	    def score(self) -> float:
30	        if self.sub_goal_completed:
31	            return 1.0
32	        if self.goal_advanced:
33	            return 0.7
34	        if self.criterion_met:
35	            return 0.4
36	        return 0.0
37	
38	
39	@dataclass
40	class LongTermOutcome:
41	    """t=days-months: Business impact."""
42	    business_goal_achieved: bool = False
43	    metric_name: str = ""
44	    metric_value: float = 0.0
45	
46	    def score(self) -> float:
47	        if self.business_goal_achieved:
48	            return 1.0
49	        return max(0.0, min(1.0, self.metric_value))
50	
51	
52	class DecisionScorer:
53	    """Scores actions using the 3-layer temporal business outcome model."""
54	
55	    def score(
56	        self,
57	        action_succeeded: bool,
58	        results_summary: str,
59	        error_message: str | None,
60	        goal_advanced: bool,
61	    ) -> float:
62	        """Compute composite score across all populated layers."""
63	        immediate = ImmediateOutcome(
64	            action_succeeded=action_succeeded,
65	            results_produced=results_summary,
66	            error_message=error_message,
67	        )
68	
69	        # Short-term and long-term are populated later via webhooks/callbacks
70	        # For immediate scoring, we only have the immediate layer.
71	        immediate_weight = 0.6
72	        short_term_weight = 0.3
73	        long_term_weight = 0.1
74	
75	        score = immediate.score() * immediate_weight
76	
77	        if goal_advanced:
78	            short_term = ShortTermOutcome(goal_advanced=True)
79	            score += short_term.score() * short_term_weight
80	            # Normalize: only immediate + short_term populated
81	            return score / (immediate_weight + short_term_weight)
82	
83	        return score / immediate_weight

8	/// The abstract browser interface.
9	///
10	/// Every browser engine (Chromium CDP, Servo, Firefox, WebKit) implements
11	/// this trait. The rest of the system never touches a concrete backend —
12	/// it only calls methods on `dyn BrowserBackend`.
13	///
14	/// # Safety
15	///
16	/// Implementations must guarantee that calling [`close`](Self::close) (or
17	/// dropping the backend) terminates the underlying browser process.
18	/// Zombie processes are a hard bug.
19	#[async_trait]
20	pub trait BrowserBackend: Send + Sync {
21	    /// Navigate to a URL. Returns page metadata after load completes.
22	    async fn navigate(&self, url: &str, timeout: Duration) -> Result<PageState, BrowserError>;
23	
24	    /// Get the current page's DOM as a distilled tree.
25	    async fn get_distilled_dom(&self, mode: DistillMode) -> Result<DistilledPage, BrowserError>;
26	
27	    /// Capture a screenshot. Returns raw PNG or JPEG bytes.
28	    async fn capture_screenshot(
29	        &self,
30	        format: ImageFormat,
31	        full_page: bool,
32	    ) -> Result<Vec<u8>, BrowserError>;
33	
34	    /// Click an element by CSS selector.
35	    async fn click(&self, selector: &str) -> Result<ActionOutcome, BrowserError>;
36	
37	    /// Type text into an element identified by CSS selector.
38	    async fn type_text(&self, selector: &str, text: &str) -> Result<ActionOutcome, BrowserError>;
39	
40	    /// Select an option from a `<select>` element.
41	    async fn select_option(
42	        &self,
43	        selector: &str,
44	        value: &str,
45	    ) -> Result<ActionOutcome, BrowserError>;
46	
47	    /// Scroll the page by pixels or to a selector.
48	    async fn scroll(
49	        &self,
50	        pixels: Option<i32>,
51	        selector: Option<&str>,
52	    ) -> Result<ActionOutcome, BrowserError>;
53	
54	    /// Wait for the page to reach a stable loaded state.
55	    async fn wait_for_load(&self, timeout: Duration) -> Result<PageState, BrowserError>;
56	
57	    /// Execute arbitrary JavaScript. Returns JSON-serialized result.
58	    /// Only used for fallback operations; prefer typed CDP commands.
59	    async fn execute_script(&self, script: &str) -> Result<String, BrowserError>;
60	
61	    /// Close the browser and clean up all resources.
62	    async fn close(&self) -> Result<(), BrowserError>;
63	}

Here is the comprehensive overview of the project at `C:\Users\kusta\ILabs\visionweb`.

---

## 1. What Is This Product?

This is the **Agent Nervous System (ANS)** -- a goal-directed perception, decision, and verification architecture for AI browser agents. It is not a browser, nor is it an AI agent itself. It is an **infrastructure layer** that sits between AI agents (Claude, ChatGPT, custom agents) and web browsers, giving agents "eyes" connected through a shared "nervous system."

The core value proposition: AI agents that browse the web are fundamentally blind -- they rely on text descriptions and HTML, with no visual confirmation of what happened, no awareness of popups blocking their path, and no semantic verification that an action actually advanced their goal. ANS solves this by giving agents **five eyes** (DOM Reader, Vision Model, Page Diff, Goal Verifier, Error Detector) that all share perceptual state laterally through a nervous system, with the goal signal broadcast continuously to all of them.

**Reference document:** `C:\Users\kusta\ILabs\visionweb\agent-nervous-system.md` contains the full product vision.

**Architecture (5 layers):**
1. **External API Gateway** -- MCP server, REST API, WebSocket for external agents
2. **Agent Web** -- Purpose-built browser rendering for agent perception (not human eyes)
3. **Nervous System + 5 Eyes** -- Cross-eye awareness, signal routing, immune system (distraction filtering)
4. **Decision Intelligence Layer** -- Action scoring with a 3-layer temporal business outcome model
5. **Storage/Budget/Immune** -- LanceDB vector storage, cost tracking with circuit breaker, prompt injection defense

**Language split:**
- **Rust** -- System skeleton (daemon, DOM distillation, page diff, immune system, IPC bridge, gateway, gRPC server)
- **Python** -- Intelligence layer (vision model eye, goal verifier, error detector, cross-eye coordinator, goal decomposer, decision scoring engine)
- **TypeScript** -- Developer dashboard/CLI (zero in critical path, not yet built)

---

## 2. What LLM/AI Model Does It Use?

**No API keys, model endpoints, or provider secrets are hardcoded in the codebase.** All AI model references are speculative or documented as design intent, not implementations.

**Explicit model references found in documentation:**

| Reference | Location | Context |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| "Claude / GPT-4o" | `agent-nervous-system.md` line 217 | Vision Eye will use these for screenshot analysis |
| "Claude, ChatGPT" | `crates/ans-gateway/src/lib.rs` line 7 | MCP server targets these as consumer agents |
| "Anthropic SDK, OpenAI SDK" | `IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md` line 87 | Python intelligence layer uses these (future) |
| "all-MiniLM-L6-v2" | `ans.toml` line 61 | Embedding model for vector search (384-dim, configurable to 768-dim) |
| "WASM ML model (ONNX runtime or burn.rs)" | `V2_ROADMAP.md` line 12 | Planned v2 enhancement for the immune system |

**Current implementation status of AI integration:**
- The Vision Eye (`C:\Users\kusta\ILabs\visionweb\nerves\ans_nerves\eyes\vision.py`) is a **stub** -- it returns empty results with `page_type: "unknown"`. There is no actual LLM call.
- The Goal Decomposer (`C:\Users\kusta\ILabs\visionweb\nerves\ans_nerves\decomposer\decomposer.py`) is a **stub** -- it returns a default `GoalSpec` with no LLM invocation.
- The Python gRPC client (`C:\Users\kusta\ILabs\visionweb\nerves\ans_nerves\grpc_client.py`) is explicitly a **Phase 0 placeholder**.
- All 5 Python eyes return empty/hardcoded data. None make API calls.
- The embedding model (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`) is referenced in the config but not loaded anywhere in the current code.

**AI Agents are consumers, not part of the product.** The gateway exposes MCP tools so Claude, ChatGPT, or any MCP-compatible agent can connect to browse the web with perception. The agent itself is external.

---

** Python tests were not run in this session, but are marked as passing in the CI config.

### CI/CD
**Yes** -- GitHub Actions workflow at `C:\Users\kusta\ILabs\visionweb\.github\workflows\ci.yml`:
- Rust: format check, build, clippy, test on ubuntu-24.04, macos-14, windows-2025
- Python: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest on all 3 OSes

- Gate: all-green combined status check

### Working Demo

### Notable Artifacts
The project root contains about 30 zero-byte files with names like `0`, `5`, `{`, `value`, `String`, `bool`, `None`, `continue`, `return`, `usize`, `BudgetMode`, `DecisionRecord`, etc. These appear to be artifacts from a previous Claude Code session that was streaming output to the filesystem.

### Summary Assessment
This is an **early-stage prototype** (Phase 0/Phase 1) that has:
- A solid, compilable Rust skeleton with well-architected crate boundaries
- Meaningful unit and integration test coverage for most Rust crates
- A clear architectural vision documented in 3 detailed markdown files
- A Python intelligence layer that exists as scaffolding only (no actual AI model integration)
- **No working demo** -- the AI/LLM integration that gives the product its purpose is not yet implemented
